narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a bit riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 52% chance of not passing. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your school performance, where your Year 3 decile of 4.0 stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward challenges ahead, suggesting those later years didn't quite build the momentum top performers often show. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 4.0, reinforcing a pattern of mid-pack grades that made the model worry about sustaining the intensity law school demands. Your middle family income added another layer, as it often means fewer resources for the extra tutoring or support that can tip the scales for bar prep. Then there's your LSAT score of 22.5, which, while solid effort, signaled to the model a tougher climb in mastering the analytical demands of legal exams. Your background as a Black female student also played into historical patterns the model has seen, where systemic hurdles have nudged outcomes toward failure more often. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 fit into that narrative too, painting a picture of steady but not standout academic drive so far. Even choosing full-time study, though dedicated, didn't fully counterbalance these in the model's view, as it weighs how part-time demands sometimes correlate with better focus for some. Together, these pieces wove a story of resilience in the face of real obstacles, but one where the model predicted the odds slightly favored stumbling at the bar. The good news? This isn't destiny—many in similar spots have boosted their trajectories with targeted prep, and we're rooting for you to rewrite that ending. Keep pushing; your determination shines through.
